quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 4 Quiz 3
quiz.subtitle: The Base Angles Theorem and Its Converse
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: The Base Angles Theorem states that if two sides of a triangle are congruent, then:
options: The angles opposite those sides are congruent
The angles adjacent to those sides are congruent
The third side is also congruent
The triangle is a right triangle
questions.explanation: The Base Angles Theorem states that if two sides of a triangle are congruent, then the angles opposite those sides (the base angles) are congruent.

questions.question: In isosceles $\triangle ABC$, $AB \cong AC$. The vertex angle is $\angle A$. If $m\angle A = 44^\circ$, what is $m\angle B$?
questions.explanation: By the Base Angles Theorem, $\angle B \cong \angle C$. The sum of angles is $180^\circ$: $44^\circ + x + x = 180^\circ$, so $44^\circ + 2x = 180^\circ$, giving $2x = 136^\circ$, so $x = 68^\circ$.

questions.question: The converse of the Base Angles Theorem states that if two angles of a triangle are congruent, then:
options: The sides opposite those angles are congruent
The sides adjacent to those angles are congruent
The third angle is also congruent
questions.explanation: The converse of the Base Angles Theorem states that if two angles of a triangle are congruent, then the sides opposite those angles are congruent, making the triangle isosceles.

questions.question: In $\triangle PQR$, $m\angle P = 50^\circ$ and $m\angle Q = 50^\circ$. Which sides must be congruent?
questions.explanation: Since $\angle P \cong \angle Q$, the sides opposite these angles are congruent by the converse of the Base Angles Theorem. The side opposite $\angle P$ is $QR$, and the side opposite $\angle Q$ is $PR$. So $PR \cong QR$.

questions.question: An equilateral triangle has all three sides congruent. What must be true about its angles?
options: One angle is $90^\circ$ and the others are $45^\circ$
Two angles are congruent and the third is different
questions.explanation: By the Base Angles Theorem, if all three sides are congruent, then all three angles are congruent. Since the sum is $180^\circ$, each angle measures $60^\circ$. An equilateral triangle is also equiangular.

questions.question: In isosceles $\triangle XYZ$, $XY \cong XZ$. The base is $YZ$. If $m\angle Y = 72^\circ$, what is $m\angle X$?
questions.explanation: By the Base Angles Theorem, $\angle Y \cong \angle Z$, so $m\angle Z = 72^\circ$. The sum of angles is $180^\circ$: $m\angle X + 72^\circ + 72^\circ = 180^\circ$, so $m\angle X = 36^\circ$.

questions.question: In $\triangle DEF$, $m\angle D = (3x + 6)^\circ$ and $m\angle E = (5x - 10)^\circ$. If $\overline{DF} \cong \overline{EF}$, what is the value of $x$?
questions.explanation: Since $DF \cong EF$, the angles opposite these sides are congruent: $\angle E \cong \angle D$. So $3x + 6 = 5x - 10$. Solving: $6 + 10 = 5x - 3x$, so $16 = 2x$, giving $x = 8$.

questions.question: A triangle has angles of $60^\circ$, $60^\circ$, and $60^\circ$. What can be concluded about its sides?
options: Two sides are congruent and the third is different
The sides have no specific relationship
questions.explanation: By the converse of the Base Angles Theorem, if all three angles are congruent, then all three sides are congruent. The triangle is equilateral.

questions.question: In isosceles $\triangle RST$, the vertex angle is $\angle S$. The base angles are $(2x + 10)^\circ$ each, and $m\angle S = (x + 20)^\circ$. What is $m\angle S$?
questions.explanation: The sum of angles is $180^\circ$: $(x + 20) + (2x + 10) + (2x + 10) = 180$. Simplifying: $5x + 40 = 180$, so $5x = 140$, giving $x = 28$. Then $m\angle S = 28 + 20 = 48^\circ$.

questions.question: Which statement is true about all isosceles triangles?
options: The base angles are always acute
The vertex angle is always the largest angle
The base is always the longest side
The base angles are always congruent
questions.explanation: By the Base Angles Theorem, the angles opposite the congruent sides (the base angles) are always congruent. This is the defining angle property of all isosceles triangles.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the Base Angles Theorem, its converse, and can apply them to isosceles and equilateral triangles.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the main theorems but review identifying which angles are opposite which sides when applying the converse.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: Base Angles Theorem (congruent sides $\rightarrow$ congruent base angles), its converse (congruent angles $\rightarrow$ congruent opposite sides), and equilateral triangles have all $60^\circ$ angles.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! In an isosceles triangle, the base angles are opposite the congruent legs. If two angles are equal, the sides opposite them are equal. An equilateral triangle is always equiangular with $60^\circ$ angles.
